The Stephen reaction and the Sandmeyer reaction are two distinct reactions in organic chemistry. The Stephen reaction involves the conversion of an acyl chloride to an aldehyde using LiAlH2Cl2 (or LiAlH2Bu2), but more commonly, it's known for converting nitriles to aldehydes. The Sandmeyer reaction is used to convert aryl diazonium salts into other compounds.

## 2: Stephen Reaction
In the Stephen reaction, a nitrile group (-CN) is reduced to an aldehyde group (-CHO). This reaction is specifically useful for the synthesis of aldehydes from nitriles.

## 3: Sandmeyer Reaction
The Sandmeyer reaction involves the conversion of an aryl diazonium salt into an aryl halide or other aryl compounds using copper(I) salts as catalysts. It can produce various products like aryl chlorides, bromides, cyanides, etc., depending on the conditions.
